annotations.label: Church–Turing thesis

anchor.snippet: It states that a function on the natural numbers can be calculated by an effective method if and only if it is computable by a Turing machine

annotations.note: The Church–Turing thesis is a meta-statement about an informal notion (effective method) and is not stated as a Lean theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General recursive functions

anchor.snippet: formalized the definition of the class of general recursive functions

annotations.note: `Nat.Partrec` inductively defines the class of (partial) general recursive functions on ℕ.

annotations.label: λ-computable function

anchor.snippet: A function on the natural numbers is called λ-computable if the corresponding function on the Church numerals can be represented by a term of the λ-calculus

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of the untyped λ-calculus or Church numerals.

annotations.label: Turing computable function

anchor.snippet: a function on the natural numbers is called Turing computable if some Turing machine computes the corresponding function on encoded natural numbers

annotations.note: `TM2Computable` (and the in-time/poly-time variants) defines what it means for a function to be computed by a finite TM2 Turing machine.

annotations.label: Equivalence of computability models

anchor.snippet: proved that these three formally defined classes of computable functions coincide

annotations.note: Mathlib proves a Turing machine TM2 can simulate every `Partrec` function (and `Nat.Partrec.Code.exists_code` encodes Partrec into codes), but the λ-calculus model and a full three-way equivalence are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Effective method (Rosser)

anchor.section: Statement in Church's and Turing's words
anchor.snippet: a method each step of which is precisely predetermined and which is certain to produce the answer in a finite number of steps

annotations.note: Informal philosophical definition; not formalized.

annotations.label: Effectively calculable vs computable

anchor.section: Statement in Church's and Turing's words
anchor.snippet: the words "effectively calculable" will mean "produced by any intuitively 'effective' means whatsoever"

annotations.note: Informal terminological clarification; no Lean counterpart.

annotations.label: Statement of the thesis

anchor.section: Statement in Church's and Turing's words
anchor.snippet: The thesis can be stated as: Every effectively calculable function is a computable function

annotations.note: Restatement of the Church–Turing thesis, an informal claim with no Lean formalization.

annotations.label: Unsolvability of the Entscheidungsproblem

anchor.snippet: proved (1936) that the Entscheidungsproblem is unsolvable: there is no algorithm that can determine whether a well formed formula has a beta normal form

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the halting problem is not computable (`halting_problem`), but does not formalize the Entscheidungsproblem for first-order logic or β-normal forms.

annotations.label: Equivalence of λ-calculus and Turing machines

anchor.snippet: Turing showed that the classes of functions defined by λ-calculus and Turing machines coincided

annotations.note: Mathlib has no λ-calculus, so this equivalence is not stated.

annotations.label: Rosser's identification

anchor.snippet: All three definitions are equivalent, so it does not matter which one is used

annotations.note: Mathlib relates `Partrec` and `Nat.Partrec` (and TM via `PartrecToTM2.tr_eval`), giving two of the three identifications but not λ-definability.

annotations.label: Kleene's Thesis I

anchor.snippet: Every effectively calculable function (effectively decidable predicate) is general recursive

annotations.note: A thesis identifying an informal notion with `Nat.Partrec`; cannot be stated as a Lean theorem.

annotations.label: Theorem XXX (Kleene)

anchor.snippet: The following classes of partial functions are coextensive

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `Nat.Partrec f ↔ ∃ c, eval c = f`, giving coextensiveness with code-evaluable functions; the full multi-way coextensiveness (μ-recursive, λ-definable, etc.) is not stated.

annotations.label: Turing's thesis

anchor.snippet: Turing's thesis that every function which would naturally be regarded as computable is computable under his definition

annotations.note: Informal thesis, not a formal Lean theorem.

annotations.label: Gandy's theorem

anchor.snippet: What can be calculated by a device satisfying principles I–IV is computable

annotations.note: Gandy machines and his principles are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sieg's computor constraints

anchor.snippet: Sieg presents a series of constraints on the behavior of a computor

annotations.note: Sieg's analysis is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel absoluteness of reckonability

anchor.snippet: a function which is computable ['reckonable'] in one of the systems S i , or even in a system of transfinite type, is already computable [reckonable] in S 1

annotations.note: No formalization of reckonability across the hierarchy of systems S_i.

annotations.label: Infinite RE set contains infinite recursive subset

anchor.snippet: Each infinite recursively enumerable (RE) set contains an infinite recursive set

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `REPred` and `ComputablePred` in `Mathlib.Computability.Halting`, but the theorem that every infinite RE set has an infinite recursive subset is not present.

annotations.label: Physical Church–Turing thesis

anchor.snippet: All physically computable functions are Turing-computable

annotations.note: Physical Church–Turing thesis is an empirical claim with no Lean formalization.

annotations.label: Logarithmic slowdown of universal Turing machine

anchor.snippet: a (multi-tape) universal Turing machine only suffers a logarithmic slowdown factor in simulating any Turing machine

annotations.note: Mathlib does not have a universal Turing machine with quantitative simulation bounds.

annotations.label: Feasibility (extended Church–Turing) thesis

anchor.snippet: A probabilistic Turing machine can efficiently simulate any realistic model of computation

annotations.note: No probabilistic TM model or complexity-theoretic CT thesis in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Invariance thesis

anchor.snippet: 'Reasonable' machines can simulate each other within a polynomially bounded overhead in time and a constant-factor overhead in space

annotations.note: No formalization of the invariance thesis (polynomial/constant simulation overhead between machine models).

annotations.label: Quantum complexity-theoretic Church–Turing thesis

anchor.snippet: A quantum Turing machine can efficiently simulate any realistic model of computation

annotations.note: Mathlib has no quantum Turing machine model.

annotations.label: Busy Beaver function

anchor.snippet: This function takes an input n and returns the largest number of symbols that a Turing machine with n states can print before halting, when run with no input

annotations.note: No `BusyBeaver`-style definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Busy Beaver and the halting problem

anchor.snippet: Finding an upper bound on the busy beaver function is equivalent to solving the halting problem

annotations.note: Without a Busy Beaver definition in Mathlib, this equivalence is not formalized.
